title: Drive By

jokes.text: The fruit vendor refuses to serve Jemaine and Bret specifically because they are from New Zealand, treating it as a serious racial grievance
jokes.setup: The vendor escalates hostility toward the two customers in a way that mimics serious racial discrimination
jokes.punchline: The target of the bigotry is 'New Zealanders' — a hilariously mundane nationality that carries zero real-world stigma, making the intensity of the prejudice completely absurd
jokes.explanation: The core absurdist premise of the episode is established here. The joke works because it deploys the full grammar of real racism — refusal of service, contempt, dehumanization — against the most inoffensive imaginable target. The gap between the gravity of the performance and the triviality of the offense is where the comedy lives. Well-constructed scene that sets up a lot of payoffs later.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Don't... don't forget to get me my, uh, banana.

jokes.text: 'Neila, can you go boil this in disinfectant?' — the vendor has Bret's apple disinfected after he touched it
jokes.setup: The vendor has already refused to serve them; the apple Bret touched is treated as contaminated
jokes.punchline: The vendor calls for the apple to be boiled in disinfectant, escalating the prejudice to a comically extreme hygienic measure
jokes.explanation: Sharp escalation of the absurd premise. The specificity of 'boil in disinfectant' is funnier than a simpler dismissal would be — it implies a whole contamination protocol exists for New Zealanders. Good economy; the joke lands fast.

dialogue.speaker: Fruit Vendor
dialogue.line: Where are you from?

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: New Zealand.

dialogue.speaker: Fruit Vendor
dialogue.line: Yeah, that's what I thought.

jokes.text: 'I don't want our customers complaining that our fruit tastes like New Zealanders.'
jokes.setup: The vendor is explaining why the apple must be disinfected
jokes.punchline: The implication that New Zealanders have a detectable, fruit-contaminating taste/smell — delivered with complete sincerity as a business concern
jokes.explanation: Excellent line. The joke is economical, specific, and escalates the absurdism with a totally straight-faced business rationale. The phrase 'tastes like New Zealanders' is inherently funny because it implies a shared, recognizable flavor profile for an entire nationality, delivered as if it's a known, accepted fact. High quotability. Justifies the near-8 craft score.

dialogue.speaker: Fruit Vendor
dialogue.line: I don't serve your kind here.

jokes.text: Band meeting minutes: 'Tension in the band. Bret upset. All sorted now.' — Murray's written minutes summarizing the brief, unresolved argument
jokes.setup: The band meeting has been a petty circular argument about whether Jemaine insulted New Zealand
jokes.punchline: Murray's meeting minutes reduce the entire emotional scene to three terse, bureaucratic phrases — including 'All sorted now' when nothing is sorted
jokes.explanation: This is a great Murray joke. It's perfectly character-integrated — only Murray, the bumbling manager who runs these band meetings with absurd formality, would be taking written minutes of an argument this petty. 'All sorted now' as a false resolution delivered in the same flat bureaucratic tone as the rest is a lovely deadpan move. The economy is superb — maximum funny per word. Justifies the 8+ craft score.

dialogue.speaker: Fruit Vendor
dialogue.line: Neila, can you go boil this in disinfectant?

jokes.text: 'Something from your mom... I don't know what it is. It seems empty.' — Murray presents Bret's package as potentially empty
jokes.setup: Murray is distributing band mail; he produces a box from Bret's mom
jokes.punchline: Murray's total indifference — 'I don't know what it is. It seems empty' — about a meaningful personal package from a parent
jokes.explanation: Competent Murray deadpan. His flat delivery of 'it seems empty' about someone's parcel from home is characteristically obtuse. Functional but not a standout moment.

dialogue.speaker: Fruit Vendor
dialogue.line: I don't want our customers complaining that our fruit tastes like New Zealanders.

jokes.text: 'Oh, great. This is my favorite box.' — Bret's response to receiving what appears to be an empty box from his mom
jokes.setup: Murray says the box seems empty; Bret opens it
jokes.punchline: Bret's genuinely delighted response that it's his 'favorite box' — the box itself is the gift, which is either heartwarming or deeply sad, played deadpan
jokes.explanation: Quietly lovely joke. The ambiguity of whether Bret is being earnest or ironic is what makes it work — he seems genuinely pleased, which is both sad (his mom sent him an empty box) and sweet (he has a favorite box). Very in character for Bret's gentle, simple disposition.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Are you picking on Bret again, Jemaine?

jokes.text: 'Nothing from your mom, Jemaine.' — Murray notes, with clinical indifference, that Jemaine received nothing
jokes.setup: Murray has just given Bret a package from his mom
jokes.punchline: Murray volunteers the information that Jemaine got nothing, unprompted — making Jemaine's motherlessness into an agenda item
jokes.explanation: Sharp little moment. Murray reads it out like it's an official communiqué. The cruelty is entirely accidental — Murray genuinely thinks this is useful information to share — which is very true to his character. Jemaine's 'So?' and its slight defensiveness round it out perfectly.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: You're hurting all of us when you bring down New Zealand, okay?

jokes.text: Bret's DVD from home is actually a VHS tape; Murray corrects him and Bret says 'Dubbed video dub'
jokes.setup: Bret refers to his mom's recording as a DVD
jokes.punchline: Murray points out it's not a DVD; Bret insists it's a 'dubbed video dub' — a completely made-up, circular non-explanation delivered with total confidence
jokes.explanation: 'Dubbed video dub' is a classic Bret move — an authoritative-sounding term that means nothing. The joke is in his confidence. Solid character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Tension in the band. Bret upset. All sorted now.

jokes.text: Murray awkwardly introduces Jessica the tech support woman to the band by calling her 'tech support advisor' instead of using her name (which he doesn't yet know)
jokes.setup: Murray introduces his band to Jessica and name-checks Jemaine and Bret
jokes.punchline: For his own introduction he defaults to her job title because he doesn't know her name — 'And this is... tech support advisor' — revealing the asymmetry of his infatuation
jokes.explanation: Solid cringe comedy — the gap between Murray's evident infatuation and his failure to even learn her name is good character observation. The pause before 'tech support advisor' does a lot of work.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Oh, great. This is my favorite box.

jokes.text: 'Pretty rock and roll... the sort of thing we talk about...' — Murray tries to spin a gig-free band meeting as rock and roll while Jessica politely exits
jokes.setup: The band meeting has been revealed to be entirely empty of real activity
jokes.punchline: Murray's desperate spin — calling a meeting about nothing 'pretty rock and roll' — while Jessica is already leaving
jokes.explanation: Functional cringe comedy. Murray's obliviousness is consistent and well-played but this is a supporting beat rather than a standout joke.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Nothing from your mom, Jemaine.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: So?

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: I'm just saying, all right?

jokes.text: 'I moved in with you guys for a month. I was upset, remember?' — Murray reveals his separation from his wife, which Jemaine and Bret had completely failed to understand at the time
jokes.setup: Murray reveals he is separated; the guys seem surprised
jokes.punchline: Murray points out he literally moved in with them for a month while clearly distressed — and they never noticed or asked why
jokes.explanation: Great character joke. The revelation that Jemaine and Bret's obliviousness is so profound that Murray's month-long crisis cohabitation went unexamined is a wonderful escalation of their mutual self-absorption. Works as character comedy for all three simultaneously.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: That's not a DVD, by the way.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Yes, it is. Dubbed video dub.

jokes.text: 'You talked me down off the roof.' — Murray reveals he was on a roof during his breakdown; Bret and Jemaine slowly, unconvincingly remember
jokes.setup: Murray is trying to establish that his separation was a visible crisis
jokes.punchline: The roof detail — implying a potential suicide attempt — is dropped casually, and the guys' non-memory of it is the joke. Bret says 'Ah, yes yes' then Jemaine says 'No.'
jokes.explanation: The dark turn — implying Murray contemplated jumping from a roof — played with total deadpan lightness is a signature FotC move. Bret's fake 'Ah, yes yes' followed immediately by Jemaine's flat 'No' is perfect comic structure. The callousness reads as social obliviousness rather than cruelty, which keeps it warm. Earns near-8 craft.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: 25 years and it's already obsolete, that one. They move fast now, don't they?

jokes.text: 'That was a great episode of The Dog Show.' — Murray's non-sequitur segue away from the emotional conversation
jokes.setup: Murray has just had a serious emotional moment about his separation
jokes.punchline: He pivots to praising a TV show about dogs, erasing the gravity of the previous exchange instantly
jokes.explanation: Quick deflation joke. The specificity of 'The Dog Show' and Murray's evident sincerity about it makes it work. Competent.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: We're just having a band meeting... talking about upcoming gigs, that sort of thing.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Yeah, there's none.

jokes.text: Jemaine and Bret debate whether the fruit vendor was 'racist' or just 'a little bit rude'
jokes.setup: The vendor refused to serve them, had their apple disinfected, and told them to get lost
jokes.punchline: Bret categorizes it as 'racist and a little bit rude' — as if 'a little bit rude' is a separate, milder charge. Jemaine initially hedges with 'He was all right.'
jokes.explanation: Nicely understated. The New Zealand politeness that makes them reluctant to call clear racism by its name — Jemaine initially suggesting he was 'all right' — is good character work. 'Racist and a little bit rude' as if these are separate offenses of roughly equal severity is a fine double-bill.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: What about your wife?

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: I'm separated.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Are you?

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Are you?

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Yes!

jokes.text: SONG: 'Albi the Racist Dragon' — an apparently sincere children's TV show song about a dragon who is racist
jokes.setup: The New Zealand TV broadcast is introducing a children's program
jokes.punchline: The show is about a racist dragon — treating racism as a normal character flaw for a children's protagonist, complete with gentle musical framing
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's centrepiece comedic constructions. The joke is that 'Albi the Racist Dragon' is presented with all the earnest warmth of a real children's character — 'everyone's favorite dragon' — but his defining characteristic is racism. This is not a throwaway; the show commits to it with a full narrative. The song form, the children's-TV cadence, and the deadpan framing all elevate it. Genuinely original concept. Earns 8+ craft.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: I moved in with you guys for a month. I was upset, remember?

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Oh, is that why you were there?

jokes.text: 'Albi the Racist Dragon' song/story — opening: children's TV show framing with dragon who is explicitly racist
jokes.setup: Bret puts on a DVD (VHS) from his mom — it turns out to be a New Zealand children's TV show
jokes.punchline: The show is called 'Albi the Racist Dragon' — the name is delivered in the children's TV musical style as if racism is a neutral character trait like being shy or clumsy
jokes.explanation: Justifies very high score — 'Albi the Racist Dragon' as a children's TV show title is a brilliant concept. The comedy comes from the total deadpan sincerity of the children's TV format (cheery musical theme, classic storybook narration) applied to a character whose defining trait is racism. It's structurally similar to the Motherf**kers song but with an opposite tonal register — instead of adult anger form carrying mundane content, it's innocent children's form carrying transgressive content. The title alone is a complete joke.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: You talked me down off the roof.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Ah, yes yes.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Remember?

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: No.

jokes.text: 'What are you doing? I thought I killed you yesterday... grumbled Albi quite racistly.'
jokes.setup: The badly burned Albanian boy returns; Albi is surprised
jokes.punchline: The narrator's editorial tag '...grumbled Albi quite racistly' — adding 'quite racistly' as a tone descriptor, as if it's a normal adverb like 'grumpily'
jokes.explanation: 'Grumbled Albi quite racistly' is a standout line. The joke is structural: 'quite racistly' functioning as an adverb of manner — as if racism has degrees of intensity that a narrator would helpfully quantify — is both absurd and linguistically clever. It also parodies the narratorial voice of children's storytelling perfectly. High quotability. One of the episode's best individual lines. Earns 8+ craft.

dialogue.line: Albi, the Racist Dragon song

jokes.text: 'Get your hand off my tail, you'll make it dirty.' — Albi's response to the burned boy's comforting gesture
jokes.setup: The boy is trying to comfort Albi by touching his tail
jokes.punchline: Albi is still being racist even in this moment of vulnerability — his racism reasserts itself mid-emotional beat as a petty hygiene concern
jokes.explanation: Great character consistency joke. The payoff is that Albi's racism can't even be suppressed when someone is being kind to him — it resurfaces as a reflex. The specific framing as a dirt concern (rather than an outright slur) keeps it within the children's-TV register while still landing the satire. Strong economy.

dialogue.speaker: TV Narrator
dialogue.line: grumbled Albi quite racistly.

jokes.text: Albi cries 'a single tear which turned into a jellybean all the colors of the rainbow. And suddenly, Albi wasn't racist anymore.'
jokes.setup: The story has been building toward some kind of resolution for Albi's racism
jokes.punchline: The cure for racism is a single rainbow jellybean tear — racism is treated as a simple, fixable character quirk that dissolves instantly with one emotional moment
jokes.explanation: The jellybean callback pays off the earlier world-building. The joke is a parody of the children's-TV moral — racism is fixed with a single magical emotional moment — which satirizes the oversimplification of prejudice in children's media. The rainbow jellybean is a nice touch (all colors = all races, presumably). Solid structure.

dialogue.speaker: Albi
dialogue.line: Get your hand off my tail, you'll make it dirty.

jokes.text: Song ends: '# Albi, the Racist... #' pause '# ...Dragon #' with rider 'Well, not anymore.'
jokes.setup: The closing theme song title is expected to reprise 'Albi the Racist Dragon'
jokes.punchline: The narrator inserts 'Well, not anymore.' between 'Racist' and 'Dragon' — a tiny bureaucratic correction to the theme song, as if accuracy in signage matters
jokes.explanation: Superb comedic timing in the song structure. The pause before 'Dragon' and the interjected 'Well, not anymore' is a perfect meta-narrative correction — the show is updating its own title mid-song to reflect the character arc. Economy is exceptional: the whole moral resolution of the story is undermined/completed in three words.

dialogue.speaker: TV Narrator
dialogue.line: And suddenly, Albi wasn't racist anymore.

jokes.text: Jemaine and Bret are visibly crying at 'Albi the Racist Dragon' — a children's cartoon about a racist dragon
jokes.setup: The Albi segment has just ended with its heartwarming resolution
jokes.punchline: Both grown men are in tears over a children's TV show about racism — completely earnest, with Bret defending it with 'Yeah. So what?'
jokes.explanation: The emotional sincerity of the response is the joke — these two are genuinely moved by a cartoon dragon's racism arc. Bret's defiant 'Yeah. So what?' suggests he considers this a totally reasonable response. Good character work for both.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Are you crying over the kids' show?

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Yes.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: So?

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: So?

jokes.text: Reaction shot: Bret is crying at Albi the Racist Dragon. 'Are you crying over the kids' show?' / 'Yes. So?'
jokes.setup: The Albi story has concluded
jokes.punchline: Bret has been moved to tears by the anti-racism dragon children's story — and defends this with 'So?' — the defensiveness suggesting he's aware it's unusual
jokes.explanation: Good character beat — Bret's emotional response to Albi is perfectly in character (he's the gentler of the two). 'Yes. So?' is a nice defiant deadpan.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: there's a lot of 'prejudism' here

jokes.text: Dave explains that New Zealanders are 'the most disliked race in this whole country' due to being 'the English and whatnot... Redcoats... the oppressors'
jokes.setup: Jemaine and Bret ask if the fruit vendor's behavior is normal
jokes.punchline: Dave explains their persecution by misidentifying them as English/Redcoats — importing colonial grievances — and escalating to claim every group in America hates them
jokes.explanation: Dave's confident wrongness is his defining comedic trait. His explanation of why New Zealanders are hated is entirely invented but delivered with authority. The 'Redcoats/oppressors' reasoning reveals he's just generically applying anti-British sentiment to the wrong country.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: You know, the... the English and whatnot. Redcoats... the oppressors. We're not English...

jokes.text: 'They don't like you either. Neither do the Chinese, the Asians, the Polish, Russian, Croatians, even the Indians.'
jokes.setup: Jemaine asks what about Black people in response to the 'most disliked' claim
jokes.punchline: Dave confirms Black people don't like them either — then lists every other group he can think of, turning an answer into an escalating catalog of enemies
jokes.explanation: Good escalation. The list keeps growing — 'even the Indians' is a good button because Dave himself is Indian, which sets up the next exchange. The 'Chinese, the Asians' redundancy is a nice absurdist touch.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: you're pretty much the most disliked race in this whole country.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Wh... wh... what about black people?

jokes.text: Jemaine and Bret are directed to the back of a bus by a driver — a direct visual parody of Rosa Parks-style segregation, applied to New Zealanders
jokes.setup: The episode has established that New Zealanders face discrimination in America
jokes.punchline: The discrimination escalates to a literal civil-rights-era visual: 'Back of the bus' — treating New Zealanders as a historically oppressed group
jokes.explanation: A visual joke that requires the audience to recognize the civil-rights reference. The absurdism of applying that specific historical injustice to New Zealanders is the point — the discrimination they face is framed with the full iconographic weight of serious civil rights history. Plays better visually than in transcript.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: They don't like you either.

jokes.text: 'Why don't you get an apple from someone who doesn't hate our country, Bret?' — Jemaine's commentary during the montage
jokes.setup: Bret is trying to buy an apple at a different vendor
jokes.punchline: Jemaine's suggestion to find a non-anti-New-Zealand apple vendor — treating this as a simple consumer tip, as if 'doesn't hate our country' is a normal product filter
jokes.explanation: Competent transitional joke. The matter-of-fact tone of 'someone who doesn't hate our country' as a basic shopping criterion is mildly amusing.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Yeah, but Dave, you're Indian. You hate us?

dialogue.speaker: Dave
dialogue.line: Yeah. Sometimes.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: But you're our best friend.

dialogue.speaker: Dave
dialogue.line: I know.

jokes.text: 'Hey, Neila! Can we bleach this one up? It's been infected!'
jokes.setup: The first vendor had the apple disinfected; this vendor escalates to bleaching
jokes.punchline: The new vendor uses different language — 'bleach this one up' — but mirrors the original's decontamination protocol, implying an industry standard for handling New Zealand-touched fruit
jokes.explanation: Callback/escalation. 'Bleach this one up' versus 'boil in disinfectant' — the variation on the same decontamination joke works but is less fresh than the original.

dialogue.speaker: Bus Driver
dialogue.line: You can't sit there, gotta go to the back.

jokes.text: 'You can buy apples from over there... my special New Zealand section.' / 'There's a dog in the New Zealand section.' / 'Well, it's for dogs from America and people from New Zealand.'
jokes.setup: The vendor directs them to a segregated New Zealand section
jokes.punchline: The New Zealand section is shared with dogs — and the vendor's explanation that it serves 'dogs from America and people from New Zealand' places New Zealanders explicitly below American dogs in the social hierarchy
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best gags. The architecture of the joke is perfect: the vendor creates a 'New Zealand section' (segregation callback), Bret notes there's a dog in it (escalation), and the vendor's explanation explicitly ranks New Zealanders below American dogs. The three-beat structure — setup, observation, clarification — is textbook. 'Dogs from America and people from New Zealand' is a great quotable line. Earns near-8 craft.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: There's a dog in the New Zealand section.

jokes.text: 'Yeah, you're being racist.' / 'How dare you call me a racist!' / 'He doesn't mean racist. He means xenophobic.' / 'Oh. Well, you're being xenophobic.'
jokes.setup: Jemaine calls the vendor racist
jokes.punchline: Bret helpfully corrects the terminology to 'xenophobic'; the vendor is immediately appeased by the more precise term and accepts the charge
jokes.explanation: Excellent exchange. The joke works on multiple levels: it satirizes people who are offended by accurate labels for their behavior, the vendor's semantic concern (racist vs. xenophobic) while continuing the exact behavior is a perfect absurdist distinction, and Bret's earnest helpfulness in correcting the terminology is perfectly in character. The structure — offense, correction, acceptance — is tight and earns 8+ craft. Very quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Fruit Vendor
dialogue.line: Well, it's for dogs from America and people from New Zealand.

jokes.text: 'Are you counting in your head?' 'Yeah.' 'What are you up to?' 'Seven.' 'Oh.'
jokes.setup: The vendor has said he'll count to 10 before things escalate
jokes.punchline: Jemaine asks for a progress update on the count; the vendor reports 'Seven' with full sincerity; Bret accepts this as useful information
jokes.explanation: Lovely little exchange. The deadpan check-in on the silent internal count — 'What are you up to?' / 'Seven' — treats the countdown as a bureaucratic process requiring status updates. All three parties engage with complete sincerity. Economy is exceptional. The 'Oh.' from Bret acknowledging the situation has progressed is perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Fruit Vendor
dialogue.line: How dare you call me a racist!

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: He doesn't mean racist. He means xenophobic.

jokes.text: 'Yeah, get your shitty bikes and get out of here before I peel your beard off!'
jokes.setup: The vendor has finished counting and issues his ultimatum
jokes.punchline: 'Peel your beard off' — a threat that is simultaneously menacing in tone and completely absurd in content; also 'shitty bikes' as a specific chosen insult
jokes.explanation: 'Peel your beard off' is a genuinely weird and specific threat that gets its laugh from the total commitment of the vendor to an anatomically improbable form of violence. 'Shitty bikes' as an insult targeting their transport is also a nice choice.

dialogue.speaker: Fruit Vendor
dialogue.line: You want a brown delicious to the face?

jokes.text: SONG: 'Too Many Motherf**kers' — Jemaine raps an explicit hip-hop song about bureaucratic frustration (bank fees) and fruit-stand racism, with bleeped profanity
jokes.setup: The guys have been repeatedly harassed at fruit stands; the situation is escalating
jokes.punchline: Jemaine's response is a professionally produced, bleeped hip-hop track in which the 'motherf**kers' interfering with his 'shi*' are primarily: (1) his bank charging ATM fees, and (2) a racist fruit vendor
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's defining comedic set pieces and one of the best FotC songs. The joke operates on several simultaneous levels: (1) the form — NWA-style explicit hip-hop — is maximally incongruous with the content — ATM transaction fees and A.P. payments; (2) the bleeped profanity creates a rhythm joke where the blanks are funnier than the words; (3) the shift from bank fees to fruit-vendor racism within the same rap is perfect tonal comedy; (4) it's an actually well-constructed rap with real internal rhyme. 'I pay my motherf**king rent fortnightly' is an exceptional lyric — 'fortnightly' is the most New Zealand word imaginable in an American rap context. Earns 8.5+ craft.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: I don't. Bret was sort of driving this situation.

jokes.text: 'I pay my motherf**king rent fortnightly' — within the rap song
jokes.setup: Jemaine is rapping about financial frustrations in aggressive hip-hop style
jokes.punchline: 'Fortnightly' — a distinctly New Zealand/British billing period word dropped into an American rap idiom, creating maximum cultural dissonance
jokes.explanation: The single best word in the episode. 'Fortnightly' in an American-style rap verse is a perfect distillation of the show's entire comedic DNA: the gap between the aggressive American cultural form and the polite New Zealand sensibility within it. It couldn't be funnier if it tried. Character integration is near-perfect — only a New Zealander rapping in America would say 'fortnightly.' Maximum economy. One of FotC's most quoted single-word gags.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: You... are you counting in your head?

dialogue.speaker: Fruit Vendor
dialogue.line: Yeah.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: What are you up to?

dialogue.speaker: Fruit Vendor
dialogue.line: Seven.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Oh.

jokes.text: 'The motherf**ker runs a racist f**king grocery / The motherf**ker won't sell an apple to a Kiwi'
jokes.setup: The rap has shifted from bank fees to the fruit vendor discrimination
jokes.punchline: The same furious rap energy that was directed at ATM fees is now applied to the apple situation — treating both grievances as equally deserving of NWA-level outrage
jokes.explanation: The word 'Kiwi' in an NWA-style rap verse is inherently funny for the same reasons as 'fortnightly.' The moral equivalence between bank fees and apple-denial as rap grievances is the sustained joke of the song.

dialogue.line: Too Many Mutha'uckers song

jokes.text: Rap threatens to 'juice the motherf**ker' and promises 'he's gonna wake up in a smoothie'
jokes.setup: Jemaine is delivering fruit-themed threats in the manner of a gangsta rap
jokes.punchline: 'Wake up in a smoothie' — a violence threat expressed entirely through fruit processing metaphors, culminating in the most benign possible image
jokes.explanation: 'Wake up in a smoothie' is a brilliant fruit-pun threat. The joke is that the escalating violence threatened in a rap chorus resolves into the most domestic, breakfast-y possible image. 'Gonna juice the motherf**ker' is also strong. The fruit metaphor sustained throughout the violent section of the rap is conceptually tight. High quotability.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: I pay my mother... ucking rent fortnightly

jokes.text: 'We're in the middle of a race war, Murray.' / 'What's that?' / 'Bad. We're in the middle of a race war.'
jokes.setup: Murray returns; Jemaine tells him things are 'pretty bad'
jokes.punchline: Murray asks what a race war is — a phrase he apparently doesn't know — and Jemaine's definition is simply 'Bad.' The escalation from apple dispute to 'race war' is played completely straight
jokes.explanation: Murray not knowing what a race war is — and Jemaine defining it as simply 'Bad' — is perfectly Murray. His confusion is endearing rather than offensive. Jemaine's flat, informational tone of 'Bad. We're in the middle of a race war' is excellent deadpan.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Then... uckin' pop an apple in his ass, yeah

jokes.text: 'We've been too busy with the race war.' / 'We're in a race war.' / 'All right!' — Murray's excited response
jokes.setup: Murray asks if they've written any songs; they cite the race war
jokes.punchline: Murray responds 'All right!' — with the enthusiasm of someone who has been told there's a great excuse for no gig progress, rather than alarm at his bandmates being in a race war
jokes.explanation: Murray's 'All right!' is perfectly in character: his managerial instinct is to accept 'race war' as a legitimate reason for no band progress, the way you might accept 'my dog died.' His obliviousness to the gravity of the situation is classic Murray.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: We're in the middle of a race war, Murray.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: What's that?

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Bad.

jokes.text: 'We've been too busy with the race war.' / 'We're in a race war.' / 'All right!' / 'It's very time consuming.'
jokes.setup: Murray asks if they've written any songs
jokes.punchline: The race war is given as an excuse for not writing songs — and Murray's 'All right!' suggests he accepts this as a legitimate reason. 'It's very time consuming' is the final deadpan button.
jokes.explanation: 'It's very time consuming' as a description of being in a race war is excellent FotC deadpan — treating an apocalyptic social event as a scheduling inconvenience. Murray's acceptance is also very good character work.

dialogue.speaker: Jessica
dialogue.line: It is on.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Oh! Oh, it just comes on! That's weird.

jokes.text: Murray's computer is 'already on' — he called tech support because he couldn't figure out how to turn on a computer that was already running
jokes.setup: Murray asked Jessica to come look at his computer because he couldn't turn it on
jokes.punchline: Jessica says 'It is on' — Murray's 'Oh! Oh, it just comes on! That's weird.' reveals he had no idea the computer was already running
jokes.explanation: Well-timed revelation about Murray's complete tech incompetence. The setup (Murray calling Jessica for tech help) pays off with the most basic possible computer problem. 'It just comes on! That's weird.' is a great character line — his genuine wonder at this is perfectly Murray.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: I can't really put my emotions into... into words. So I've decided to use lyrics.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Well, this... they're also words.

jokes.text: 'Is it hot in here?' — Murray, after Jessica leaves, clearly flustered by attraction
jokes.setup: Jessica has just solved Murray's non-existent computer problem and left
jokes.punchline: Murray's immediate, slightly confused reaction to her presence — 'Is it hot in here?' — confirming his infatuation while being completely unaware of how transparent he is
jokes.explanation: Competent reaction beat but 'Is it hot in here?' is a fairly well-worn shorthand for attraction. Gets the job done but is not a distinguished joke.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Hi.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: I like it.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Yeah?

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: 'Hi'?

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Yep.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Is that it?

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Yeah.

jokes.text: Murray's love song consists of one word: '# Hi. #'
jokes.setup: Murray has been working on a love song for Jessica and finally shares his progress
jokes.punchline: The entirety of his song is the word 'Hi' — which he sings with apparent pride
jokes.explanation: Perfect joke. The setup — Murray asking two professional songwriters to help him with his love song — builds anticipation for something embarrassing. The delivery of '# Hi. #' as the entirety of his work is a superb deflation. It's also perfectly in character: Murray is a man of minimal self-expression who considers this sufficient. Jemaine and Bret's responses — 'I like it' and 'Is that it?' — mirror the audience's split reaction. Economy is absolute: one word is the whole joke. Earns 8+ craft.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: I don't want to make it too convoluted.

jokes.text: 'Have you thought about adding some more words?' / 'I don't know. I don't want to make it too convoluted.'
jokes.setup: Bret suggests Murray might want more than one word in his song
jokes.punchline: Murray worries that more words would make the song 'too convoluted' — he genuinely believes 'Hi' risks being over-engineered if expanded
jokes.explanation: 'I don't want to make it too convoluted' in reference to a one-word song is exceptional character comedy. Murray's aesthetic philosophy — that 'Hi' is a complete artistic statement that risks over-elaboration — is a beautifully absurd application of minimalist artistic theory to total creative bankruptcy. Highly quotable and perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: I like pie. I had a budgie but it died.

jokes.text: Murray's expanded lyrics: 'I like pie. I had a budgie but it died.'
jokes.setup: Jemaine suggests Murray add more words; Murray shares his second draft
jokes.punchline: He's added 'I like pie' (generic) and 'I had a budgie but it died' (extremely specific, sad, and completely inappropriate for a love song) — and considers these equal additions
jokes.explanation: The 'budgie' line is a standout. The joke is the tonal collision: 'I like pie' is a harmless non-sequitur, but 'I had a budgie but it died' is a genuine personal tragedy that Murray has chosen to open with in a love song. The fact that Murray considers this an emotionally valid inclusion ('it brings up emotions for me') makes it even funnier. High character integration — this is exactly the kind of disclosure Murray would think was appropriate.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: # Hot leggy blonde chick, got it going on # Wanna see you wearing that thong thong thong... # ...Panties on

jokes.text: 'I'm shy. I'm a guy. I wear ties.' — Murray's rhyme section
jokes.setup: Murray shares more lyrics from his evolving love song
jokes.punchline: 'I wear ties' as the climax of a rhyme scheme about personal identity — self-describing his formal office wear as a romantic credential
jokes.explanation: The escalation from 'I'm shy / I'm a guy' (attempting emotional authenticity) to 'I wear ties' (a strictly factual, unromantic detail) is the joke. Murray clearly thinks this is a compelling revelation. Very in character for Murray, who is always wearing ties.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: it's inappropriate. I can't say that in the workplace, can I?

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Too much mumbling. Yeah, a lot of it was inaudible.

jokes.text: Murray's notes on Jessica: 'She's got great legs... great hair... smokin' hot. Whew.' — Jemaine and Bret react with increasing enthusiasm
jokes.setup: Murray shares his observational notes about Jessica
jokes.punchline: The increasingly frat-bro energy of the descriptors — culminating in 'smokin' hot. Whew.' — contrasted with Murray's normal prim managerial persona; Jemaine and Bret enthusiastically endorsing each claim
jokes.explanation: Decent escalation gag. The enthusiasm is infectious but the joke is fairly conventional workplace-attraction territory for this show.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Ah, Jessica. That's her name.

jokes.text: Jemaine spontaneously offers lyrics: '# Hot leggy blonde chick, got it going on / Wanna see you wearing that thong thong thong... panties on #'
jokes.setup: Murray wants help writing something for Jessica; Jemaine offers to contribute
jokes.punchline: Jemaine's contribution is immediately inappropriate, explicit, and a clear Nelly 'Hot in Herre' parody — not suitable for a workplace colleague
jokes.explanation: This song fragment sets up its own callback at the end of the episode when it's incorporated into 'Leggy Blonde.' Funny in the moment as a workplace-inappropriate Jemaine contribution; funnier in retrospect. The mumbling complaint from Murray ('Too much mumbling') is a nice undercut.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Do you have the new Gipsy Kings cassette?

dialogue.speaker: Library Worker
dialogue.line: Let me check. Yes, we have it.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: I would like to reserve it.

dialogue.speaker: Library Worker
dialogue.line: I'll put it aside for you.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Excellent. Goodbye.

jokes.text: Murray's critique of Jemaine's lyrics: 'It's inappropriate... Too much mumbling. Yeah, a lot of it was inaudible.'
jokes.setup: Jemaine has just performed an explicit song about Jessica
jokes.punchline: Murray's primary objection is not the content but the audibility — his complaint is that it was hard to hear, as if the main problem with an explicit workplace song is poor enunciation
jokes.explanation: Very good Murray line. The misplacement of his concern — mumbling over inappropriate content — is a perfect expression of his character. He adds 'You can't say that in the workplace, can I?' almost as an afterthought, revealing he knows it's wrong but his primary concern is the technical quality.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: With telephones, you make the call.

jokes.text: Murray reverts to '# Hi. #' as his final song, satisfied
jokes.setup: After all the discussion of lyrics — budgie deaths, rhymes, Jemaine's explicit contribution — Murray circles back
jokes.punchline: He returns to the single-word song, having absorbed no feedback, still singing '# Hi. #' with the same satisfaction
jokes.explanation: The callback to '# Hi. #' after the whole extended songwriting session having changed nothing is a perfect structural payoff. Murray's satisfaction with his original vision, unchanged and unimproved, is completely in character. The scene has done a full circle and all the discussion was for nothing — which is exactly right.

dialogue.speaker: Dave
dialogue.line: Inject strychnine into a piece of his fruit. Then when a customer dies, the C.I.A. Will trace it back to the vendor and he's going straight to the big house... Alcatraz.

jokes.text: Murray sends Bret and Jemaine out of his office so he can summon Jessica — 'Go on, out you go! Meeting over.'
jokes.setup: Murray has a plan — pretend his computer needs fixing again
jokes.punchline: He abruptly ends the meeting and herds the band out with the same managerial authority he uses to run band meetings — pivoting from band manager to lovestruck teenager in one motion
jokes.explanation: Mild beat. The joke is in Murray's authority being used for personal romantic ends, which is consistent but not particularly elevated. Functional.

dialogue.speaker: Dave
dialogue.line: sometimes what I do is I think mean thoughts about the person. How do they know? They don't know.

jokes.text: Music library phone call: 'Do you have the new Gipsy Kings cassette?' — a random interlude of Murray's NZ consulate 'music library' function
jokes.setup: Murray is at work in what is ostensibly the New Zealand consulate
jokes.punchline: The consulate apparently operates a lending library for cassette tapes, and someone is calling to reserve the Gipsy Kings — which they have
jokes.explanation: Quietly funny world-building joke. The New Zealand consulate having a cassette lending library — and Murray handling it with formal professionalism — adds to the show's ongoing joke about Murray's absurd institutional role. The Gipsy Kings specificity and 'cassette' (not CD) are good choices.

dialogue.speaker: Dave
dialogue.line: Say a comeback to him!

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Banana balls! You look like a... a something... like a banana balls.

jokes.text: 'With telephones, you make the call.' — Murray's closing observation after the phone call
jokes.setup: Murray has just completed a perfectly routine phone call
jokes.punchline: Murray offers this as if it's a profound insight — 'With telephones, you make the call' — a tautology delivered as wisdom
jokes.explanation: This is a great Murray non-sequitur. 'With telephones, you make the call' is a tautology presented as a life observation — as if Murray has just discovered something profound about the telephone. The deadpan delivery of an empty statement as wisdom is perfectly Murray. Economy is near-perfect: six words. Earns 8 craft.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: What is the bird? What's that?

dialogue.speaker: Dave
dialogue.line: That's the bird.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Well, that bird doesn't have any wings. It's only got one leg.

jokes.text: Dave's revenge plan: inject strychnine in the fruit, the CIA traces it back, vendor goes to 'the big house... Alcatraz'
jokes.setup: The guys want to get back at the vendor; Dave offers a plan
jokes.punchline: Dave's scheme — poisoning customers to frame the vendor — is presented as a reasonable escalation, and Dave genuinely references Alcatraz as if it's still operational
jokes.explanation: Dave's willingness to suggest murder-by-strychnine as a proportionate response to an apple dispute is good character comedy. 'The big house... Alcatraz' is funny because Alcatraz closed in 1963 — Dave's information is outdated. The casual trajectory from apple refusal to poisoning civilians is the Dave character in miniature.

dialogue.speaker: Dave
dialogue.line: try putting some wings on your bird. There we go. That's much nicer. But you don't wanna be nice.

jokes.text: 'Haven't you seen Escape From Alcatraz? It wasn't easy, bro.'
jokes.setup: Bret says he doesn't want to kill anyone; Dave is trying to persuade him
jokes.punchline: Dave's counter-argument against the prison plan is not 'murder is wrong' but 'escaping Alcatraz is hard' — his concern is the practicality of prison escape, not the ethics of poisoning people
jokes.explanation: Excellent character joke. Dave's entire moral framework is revealed: his hesitation about the strychnine plan is entirely practical (hard to escape Alcatraz) rather than ethical (murder is bad). The 'bro' at the end — his casual buddy-register while discussing mass poisoning logistics — makes it funnier. Earns 8+ craft through perfect character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Dave
dialogue.line: It's not a fucking school play production. It's the bird.

jokes.text: 'Sometimes what I do is I think mean thoughts about the person.' / 'How do they know?' / 'They don't know. I know, that's why I didn't suggest it.'
jokes.setup: Dave is listing revenge options from most to least drastic
jokes.punchline: Jemaine volunteers his personal method: thinking mean thoughts — he starts to share it, then immediately acknowledges it doesn't work since no one can detect his thoughts, which is why he 'didn't suggest it' (he did just suggest it)
jokes.explanation: Very good exchange. Jemaine's suggestion of thinking mean thoughts as a revenge tactic — and his self-defeating observation that 'They don't know. I know, that's why I didn't suggest it' immediately after suggesting it — is a perfect self-undermining bit. The logic is completely circular and Jemaine is completely serious. Strong structure and economy.

dialogue.speaker: Fruit Vendor
dialogue.line: Throw another shrimp on the barbie. Ride around on your kangaroos all day.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: No no no no.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: That's Australians.

jokes.text: Dave's revision of his advice: 'something a little more subtle than framing him for murder — which I actually think is still a great idea — but more obvious than thinking mean thoughts about him'
jokes.setup: Dave is calibrating the appropriate revenge response on a spectrum
jokes.punchline: He describes framing the vendor for murder as 'subtle' relative to thinking mean thoughts — placing 'frame for murder' somewhere in the middle of the revenge spectrum, while insisting the murder frame is 'still a great idea'
jokes.explanation: The joke is the spectrum: framing someone for murder is 'more subtle' than thinking mean thoughts — Dave's scale of proportionality is completely inverted from a normal person's. His parenthetical '— which I actually think is still a great idea —' is perfect: he can't let the murder plan go.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: The accents are completely different. Th... they're like, 'Where's the car?' And we're like, 'Where's the car?'

jokes.text: Vendor shouts racist stereotypes from across the street: 'New Zealanders are all unhygienic!' / 'New Zealanders all have tartar on their teeth!'
jokes.setup: The guys are discussing revenge strategies; the vendor is outside shouting at them
jokes.punchline: The vendor's stereotypes about New Zealanders are completely invented — hygiene and tartar — as if he's improvising anti-NZ prejudice on the spot, making up grievances that have no cultural basis whatsoever
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist extension of the premise. The stereoypes the vendor invents — dental hygiene, general uncleanliness — are random enough to feel improvised rather than culturally loaded, which is exactly the point: this hatred is entirely invented.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Hi, leggy blonde...

jokes.text: 'Hey, what are you guys doing? You, uh, you bungee jumping up there?' — vendor's attempt at an anti-New Zealand taunt
jokes.setup: The vendor is trying to insult them with New Zealand stereotypes
jokes.punchline: Bungee jumping — a New Zealand invention — is the specific cultural reference the vendor lands on, but it's such a mild, positive association that it barely registers as an insult
jokes.explanation: The bungee jumping 'insult' is funny because it's the best anti-New Zealand stereotype the vendor can muster, and it's not remotely offensive. The confused denial — 'No. No, we're not.' — from the guys treats it as an equally serious accusation.

dialogue.speaker: Greg
dialogue.line: she's no longer here.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Oh, that's fine. We'll do it in the morning.

dialogue.speaker: Greg
dialogue.line: No, I mean she's finished installing the new computer system. She's not coming back.

jokes.text: 'Banana balls! / You look like a... a something... like a banana balls.' — Bret's attempted comeback
jokes.setup: Dave urges Bret to insult the vendor back; Bret attempts a comeback
jokes.punchline: Bret's comeback is 'Banana balls' — a meaningless, improvised non-insult delivered with genuine effort, followed by his attempt to clarify it as a simile ('you look like a banana balls') that makes even less sense
jokes.explanation: 'Banana balls' is a perfect Bret joke. It's the best insult his sweet, unconfrontational nature can generate — a nonsense compound noun — and his attempt to develop it into a simile only makes it worse. The vendor immediately weaponizes it: 'New Zealanders all mumble, I can't understand you' — turning Bret's terrible comeback into a new stereotype. Earns 8+ craft through character integration (only Bret could produce 'banana balls' under pressure) and the structural beauty of having it immediately backfire.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Leggy Blonde song

jokes.text: Vendor immediately turns 'banana balls' back: 'New Zealanders all mumble. I can't understand you.'
jokes.setup: Bret delivers his incoherent 'banana balls' comeback
jokes.punchline: The vendor adds 'mumbling' as a new New Zealand stereotype — instantly incorporating Bret's failed delivery into his discrimination repertoire
jokes.explanation: The vendor 'He turned it around back on us' is a perfect observation. The joke is that the vendor can spin literally anything into evidence of New Zealand inferiority — even Bret's incoherence becomes a stereotype. This is a great structural payoff to the comeback scene.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: I had a budgie but it died

jokes.text: 'Flip him the bird' — Dave's instruction; neither Jemaine nor Bret know what 'the bird' is
jokes.setup: Dave tells them to flip the bird as a final comeback option
jokes.punchline: Jemaine and Bret have no idea what 'the bird' is — the idiom is culturally unfamiliar to them — setting up the extended 'wrong finger' sequence
jokes.explanation: Setup for the extended bird tutorial sequence. The cultural unfamiliarity is a genuine character moment — New Zealanders wouldn't necessarily share American gesture idioms.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: I'll never get to tear your clothes off on the photocopier

jokes.text: Bret's 'bird': he extends the wrong finger and says 'Well, that bird doesn't have any wings. It's only got one leg.'
jokes.setup: Dave shows them the middle finger; Bret critiques it as a bird representation
jokes.punchline: Bret evaluates the gesture on ornithological grounds — it's anatomically incorrect as a bird — completely missing the point
jokes.explanation: Bret's ornithological critique of the middle finger is delightful. 'It's only got one leg' is the kind of literal, earnest response that only Bret could produce — he's genuinely trying to make it a better bird. Perfectly in character.

jokes.text: 'It's not a fucking school play production. It's the bird.' — Dave's exasperated summary
jokes.setup: Bret and Jemaine have been attempting to improve the bird into a more visually accurate or offensive version
jokes.punchline: Dave's 'school play production' comparison — implying they're treating a rude gesture like a choreographed performance requiring directorial notes
jokes.explanation: 'School play production' is a great simile for what Jemaine and Bret are doing — treating a conventional gesture as a creative collaborative project. Dave's frustration is earned.

jokes.text: Dave explains the bird's meaning: 'Hey, you better not disrespect me, man, even though I'm gonna disrespect you.'
jokes.setup: Bret asks what's offensive about the bird
jokes.punchline: Dave's explanation reveals the circular self-contradiction in the bird's message — a demand for respect delivered via disrespect
jokes.explanation: A genuinely insightful observational joke about the self-contradiction in the middle finger as a social signal. Well-articulated and lands as both a real observation and a Dave character moment.

jokes.text: Bret practices the bird with Dave coaching him through it with timing: 'Around, and up. Good, Bret. Around and up.' — one minute 32 seconds to learn
jokes.setup: Dave is teaching Bret the middle finger gesture
jokes.punchline: The coaching is delivered with the gravity and methodology of a sports coach teaching a technique — 'around and up' as a biomechanical instruction — and it still takes Bret 1 minute 32 seconds
jokes.explanation: The timing detail — 'one minute, 32 seconds' — is the button to the whole bird sequence. The investment in teaching a one-finger gesture taking nearly two minutes is a good escalation payoff. The visual of Bret earnestly working through the motion while Dave coaches is strong physical comedy.

jokes.text: 'It was either this or getting you sent into Alcatraz.' — Jemaine's explanation to the vendor of why they chose the bird over the strychnine plan
jokes.setup: The vendor confronts them about their offensive gesture; Jemaine explains
jokes.punchline: Jemaine casually reveals that the alternative under consideration was 'getting you sent into Alcatraz' — confirming the murder-frame plan was genuinely on the table
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback. Jemaine's matter-of-fact delivery — 'It was either this or getting you sent into Alcatraz' — treats the murder-frame plan as a genuine option that was seriously weighed. The vendor's reaction to this (presumably confused) and the audience's delight at the callback are both earned. Earns 8+ craft.

jokes.text: Jemaine's speech: '...Or whether they spell words slightly differently... some would say more correctly.'
jokes.setup: Jemaine is giving a sincere speech about human dignity and not judging people for being different
jokes.punchline: He cannot resist inserting a nationalist claim that New Zealand spelling is 'more correct' than American spelling — undermining the universalist message mid-delivery
jokes.explanation: Excellent character joke. Jemaine is making a sincere speech about tolerance and cannot resist qualifying it with a nationalist jab — 'some would say more correctly' — which simultaneously makes him look petty and reveals his actual priorities. The vendor's 'Let me finish' is a great straight-man beat: he's so invested in the speech that even the digression irritates him.

jokes.text: Vendor is moved by Jemaine's speech: 'That's a great speech. Too bad New Zealanders are a bunch of cocky a-holes descended from criminals and retarded monkeys.'
jokes.setup: Jemaine has given an earnest, emotional speech about human dignity and treating all people as people
jokes.punchline: The vendor praises the speech sincerely before pivoting to a full-throated reinforcement of his prejudice — making clear the speech has had zero effect
jokes.explanation: 'That's a great speech' building genuine hope before the hard pivot is solid misdirection. The 'criminals and retarded monkeys' line is harsher than most of the episode's language — it lands as a tonal escalation just before the resolution.

jokes.text: 'No, you're thinking of Australians.' — the revelation that the vendor has been confusing New Zealanders with Australians the whole time
jokes.setup: The vendor has been describing New Zealanders as 'criminals descended from monkeys,' 'Throw another shrimp on the barbie,' 'ride around on kangaroos'
jokes.punchline: All of his stereotypes apply to Australians — the vendor has been discriminating against the wrong nationality the entire episode
jokes.explanation: The episode's best structural joke. The entire episode's racism storyline resolves on the revelation that the vendor's prejudice was based on a case of mistaken national identity — he has been confusing New Zealand with Australia. This recontextualizes everything: even the framing of New Zealand as a persecuted group was based on an error. The joke works as a structural payoff, as a satire of prejudice (it's arbitrary and based on ignorance), and as a New Zealand/Australia joke. The vendor's 'I feel terrible' and 'Their accents are kinda similar' is a perfect resolution. The structure alone justifies 8+ craft.

jokes.text: 'Th... they're like, "Where's the car?" And we're like, "Where's the car?"' — Jemaine demonstrates the supposedly distinct accents
jokes.setup: The vendor says the accents are 'kinda similar'; Jemaine insists they're completely different
jokes.punchline: Jemaine's demonstration of the Australian vs New Zealand accent difference produces two phrases that sound identical (at least in subtitle; presumably nearly identical in delivery) — undermining his own argument
jokes.explanation: The joke depends on the audio (Jemaine's failed accent differentiation), but even in transcript form the structure is clear: his emphatic insistence that they're 'completely different' while producing near-identical examples is a perfect self-undermining moment. Played live, this would get a bigger laugh.

jokes.text: The vendor apologizes and gives them free fruit; they're suddenly best friends with Sinjay the vendor after the entire episode's conflict
jokes.setup: The entire episode has built toward confronting this xenophobic vendor
jokes.punchline: The conflict resolves instantly and warmly — 'I feel terrible' / 'Well, this Red Delicious is on me' — the race war ends with a free apple because it was a case of mistaken identity
jokes.explanation: Good structural payoff — the 'race war' resolves with a free piece of fruit and handshakes. The anti-climax is perfectly calibrated. 'Neila, you can uncover your eyes. They're not Australian. They're New Zealanders.' is a nice button.

jokes.text: SONG: 'Leggy Blonde' — Murray's full love song for Jessica, incorporating the budgie, the pie, and the thong lyrics as a complete musical number after she's gone
jokes.setup: Murray has been composing a love song throughout the episode using extremely limited, inappropriate, and mundane material
jokes.punchline: The 'Leggy Blonde' number incorporates every element of Murray's terrible songwriting — the budgie, the pie, the legs/hair observation, Jemaine's explicit verse — into a complete, polished musical number performed after Jessica has permanently left
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's two centrepiece songs. 'Leggy Blonde' works as a complete comedic musical payoff because it assembles every piece of Murray's terrible songwriting into a sincere, polished ABBA-esque number — and delivers it after Jessica is already gone forever. The structure is excellent: we've seen the components being assembled (budgie, pie, ties, thong), and the song delivers them as a complete, emotionally earnest piece. 'I had a budgie but it died' in the middle of the romantic chorus is perfectly incongruous. The verse 'Her hair down to her legs / And her legs down to the floor' is also a gently absurdist description of anatomy. The pathos of performing it for nobody earns its emotional beats. Earns 8.5+ craft as a complete comedic musical construction.

jokes.text: Greg delivers the news that Jessica won't return: 'She's finished installing the new computer system. She's not coming back.' Murray: 'Oh, right. She's gone.'
jokes.setup: Murray asks Greg to bring Jessica in again
jokes.punchline: Greg's matter-of-fact delivery of the romantic death notice ('She's not coming back') and Murray's flat 'She's gone' mirrors the show's register — enormous emotional content delivered as if it's a scheduling note
jokes.explanation: Good deadpan — Greg's efficient delivery of the bad news and Murray's 'She's gone' (echoing the way you'd say a temp had finished a contract) is appropriately understated given the enormity of Murray's feelings.

jokes.text: 'Her hair down to her legs / And her legs down to the floor'
jokes.setup: The 'Leggy Blonde' song is describing Jessica
jokes.punchline: The anatomical description is technically accurate but absurdly literal — hair reaching legs and legs reaching floor is just... how humans are configured — delivered as if it's an extraordinary feature
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's finest single lines. 'Her hair down to her legs / And her legs down to the floor' reads as a romantic description of an exceptional woman but is actually just a description of normal human proportions. The joke is entirely structural: these are observations that are true of almost every person. The delivery as poetic, longing romance makes the literal banality funnier. Very high quotability. Earns 8+ craft.

jokes.text: 'I had a budgie but it died' — inserted into the romantic chorus of 'Leggy Blonde'
jokes.setup: The song is in full romantic swing
jokes.punchline: Murray's budgie line — the most tonally inappropriate element of his songwriting — is inserted mid-emotional climax as a sincere autobiographical disclosure
jokes.explanation: 'I had a budgie but it died' dropped into the middle of a romantic song is the episode's single best comedic moment in the song. It has been seeded earlier (Murray said 'it brings up emotions for me') so it's earned, not random. Its placement — at the emotional peak of the song — makes it maximally incongruous. The line itself has a perfect rhythm and the specificity (budgie, not 'a bird' or 'a pet') keeps it anchored in Murray's character. Very high quotability and callback value.

jokes.text: '# I had a budgie but it died #' — appears mid-song in 'Leggy Blonde'
jokes.setup: The song has been building as a straight tribute; a new lyrical section begins
jokes.punchline: Murray's original lyric from the workshop ('I had a budgie but it died') makes it into the final song — the dead parakeet is officially part of the love song
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best moments. The callback lands with maximum force because (a) the song has earned its emotional momentum, (b) the budgie was dismissed during the workshop as inappropriate, and (c) it made it in anyway — Murray's grief for his parakeet is inextricably bound to his love for Jessica. The structural decision to pay off a workshop joke inside the performance is technically excellent. Extremely quotable. Earns 8+ easily.

jokes.text: Song: '# I like pie #' — Murray's other lyric also makes the final song
jokes.setup: The song has incorporated 'I had a budgie but it died'
jokes.punchline: 'I like pie' — Murray's generic personal statement — also appears in the final love song, completing the set of his original terrible lyrics
jokes.explanation: Justifies 8+ — the fact that both terrible Murray lyrics make it into the final song is the completion of a beautiful running gag. 'I like pie' appearing in a heartfelt farewell ballad after 'I had a budgie but it died' is structurally perfect. Murray's romantic statement to the universe: hair, legs, budgie died, pie, goodbye.

jokes.text: Final verse: 'I'll never get to tear your clothes off on the photocopier' — in the middle of the sincere, sad romantic outro
jokes.setup: The song is in its emotional outro — 'I'll never get to be with you / share a cup of tea with you / let you know how much I think of you'
jokes.punchline: The sequence of gentle, platonic regrets escalates to 'tear your clothes off on the photocopier' — a sudden explicit workplace fantasy dropped in the same gentle tone
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation/misdirection. The series of 'I'll never get to...' builds a pattern of gentle, sad regrets — tea, letting her know how he feels — then the photocopier line breaks the register entirely. The workplace specificity ('photocopier' rather than a generic location) is the right word. Structure is textbook: establish a pattern, subvert it at the peak. High quotability and impact. Earns 8+ craft.

jokes.text: Finale: Jemaine's 'thong thong thong / panties on' verse from the earlier workplace meeting is incorporated into the full 'Leggy Blonde' song as the closing verse
jokes.setup: At 13:40 Murray rejected this verse as inappropriate for a workplace colleague
jokes.punchline: The rejected verse appears as the final, triumphant close of 'Leggy Blonde' — the most inappropriate lyric becomes the musical climax
jokes.explanation: A satisfying structural callback. The verse that was explicitly rejected by Murray as inappropriate for a workplace song becomes the closing number — Murray presumably either approved it in the heat of composition or wasn't paying attention. The contrast between the sincere 'Goodbye' and the Nelly-style finale is a good tonal closer.

tmdb_overview: Bret and Jemaine are shocked by the prejudiced actions of a fruit vendor who won't sell to them because they are New Zealanders. Dave recommends a strong response and teaches them how to "flip the bird". Murray is smitten with the new tech support lady at the consulate.
